textPlain: The article ends on a note which can be read in either a hopeful or ominous key."Authorities anticipate no significant conflicts with the beaver colony for at least the next decade."
I'm Czech and haven't heard about this. We should hire more beavers for construction projects.

The project was stalled not because they didn't have funding, but because they didn't have permits.I'm increasingly of the opinion that permission-based economies don't work.  Go after people who harm, don't impose paperwork to preemptively prevent harm.
We have always been at war with Eurasian beavers (Castor fiber).

I learned a while back that Hammurabi's Code contained a proto-building code, in that a builder whose building collapsed and harmed its occupant could be punished in a similar way.However the occupant was still harmed.Individuals must have a complete context for the environment in which they operate in order to build "correctly." Permit reviews, building codes etc. codify that context so that builders don't need to carry the entire system context, even if they could, which often they can't. And as a result, buildings don't randomly fall over or catch fire with nearly the same frequency as they used to
This doesn't seem like a very sensible policy. There are a great many harms that once done, cannot be undone.
